questions.text: Le Chatelier Principle is about:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Reaction mixture is the combination of reactants and products in a chemical reaction. It is the system that is at equilibrium.
Reactants are the substances that are present at the start of a chemical reaction. They are converted into products by the reaction.

Le Chatelier's Principle states that: when a system at equilibrium is subjected to an external stress, the system will adjust in such a way as to partially counteract the effect of the stress and restore equilibrium. The principle applies to changes in temperature, pressure, and concentration. By focusing on the equilibrium mixture, it emphasizes that the system will respond to any changes in the concentrations of reactants or products by shifting the equilibrium position to minimize the effect of the change and maintain equilibrium.
Products are the substances that are formed by a chemical reaction. They are produced from the reactants.

questions.explanation: Le Chatelier’s Principle describes how an equilibrium system responds to changes in temperature, pressure, or concentration. It predicts the direction in which the equilibrium will shift to counter the applied change. Therefore, it is specifically about the behavior of an equilibrium mixture.

questions.text: In contact process, to which substance adequate quantities of water is added to convert it to sulphuric acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is oleum, or fuming sulfuric acid, which is formed when SO3 is dissolved in H2SO4. It is the correct answer, as it requires water to be converted into dilute sulfuric acid.

This is the hydrogen sulfate ion, a product of sulfuric acid when it donates a proton. While related to sulfuric acid, it is not directly involved in the contact process as a precursor to sulfuric acid.

This is sulfur trioxide, which reacts with water to form sulfuric acid. However, this reaction is highly exothermic and not typically the method used in the contact process for producing sulfuric acid.
This is elemental sulfur, which is initially transformed into sulfur dioxide in the contact process. It is not the compound that is directly converted into sulfuric acid.

questions.explanation: In the contact process, SO₃ is not added directly to water because it forms a mist that is hard to absorb. Instead, SO₃ is first absorbed in concentrated H₂SO₄ to form oleum (H₂S₂O₇). Adding adequate water to oleum safely produces sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄).

questions.text: Which factor does affect the equilibrium constant Kc for a reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Changing the temperature shifts the equilibrium position based on whether the reaction is endothermic (absorbs heat) or exothermic (releases heat), according to Le Chatelier's Principle.3 Since the ratio of products to reactants changes, the value of K must also change.
B is incorrect because pressure changes shift equilibrium position, not the Kc value, which depends only on temperature for a given reaction.
C is incorrect because catalysts speed up reaching equilibrium but do not alter the Kc value, which is temperature‑dependent only in chemistry.
D is incorrect because initial concentrations determine the reaction quotient, not the Kc, which is a fixed temperature‑dependent constant for each reaction.

questions.explanation: Changing the temperature shifts the equilibrium position based on whether the reaction is endothermic (absorbs heat) or exothermic (releases heat), according to Le Chatelier's Principle.3 Since the ratio of products to reactants changes, the value of K must also change.

questions.text: According to the law of mass action, "The rate of chemical reaction" is proportional to the:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the law of mass action does not directly relate to the total amount of product formed, but rather to the concentrations of the reactants.

options.text: Product of the molar concentration of the reactant
options.explanation: This option is correct as it accurately reflects the law of mass action, which states that the rate of a reaction is proportional to the product of the concentrations of the reactants raised to the power of their coefficients in the balanced equation.
This option is incorrect because the law of mass action involves the concentrations of both reactants and products at any point in time, not just the initial concentrations.

This option is incorrect because a catalyst affects the rate of a reaction but does not appear in the expression of the law of mass action, which focuses solely on reactant and product concentrations.

questions.explanation: The law of mass action states that reaction rate depends on how frequently reactant molecules collide. This frequency increases with their molar concentrations, so the rate is proportional to their product. Thus, option B correctly describes the law of mass action.

questions.text: At equilibrium, which of the following reactions is not affected by pressure?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This reaction involves the formation of nitrogen monoxide (NO) from nitrogen gas (N2) and oxygen gas (O2). Since the stoichiometric coefficients of all the gases in the balanced equation are equal, a change in pressure will not affect the equilibrium position. The correct option is A.

In this reaction, phosphorus pentachloride (PCl5) decomposes into phosphorus trichloride (PCl3) and chlorine gas (Cl2). The balanced equation contains gaseous reactants, so changes in pressure will affect the equilibrium position.

This reaction involves the equilibrium between nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and dinitrogen tetroxide (N2O4). The stoichiometric coefficients are not equal for the reactants and products, so changes in pressure will affect the equilibrium position. An increase in pressure will favor the formation of N2O4, while a decrease in pressure will favor the formation of NO2.

This reaction represents the equilibrium between thionyl chloride (SOCl2), sulfur dioxide (SO2), and chlorine gas (Cl2). The stoichiometric coefficients are not equal for the reactants and products, so changes in pressure will affect the equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: Pressure affects only reactions where the number of gas moles changes. In option A, reactants have 1 mole of gas total (0.5 + 0.5), and the product also has 1 mole. Since gas moles are equal on both sides, pressure has no effect on this equilibrium.

questions.text: If the initial ratio of products to reactants(the reaction quotient) is greater than the Kc then:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The reaction will shift towards the reverse direction.
options.explanation: Correct! When the initial reaction quotient (Q) is greater than the equilibrium constant (Kc), the system is product-heavy, and the reaction shifts to the left to reach equilibrium by forming more reactants.

options.text: More quantity of products is obtained.
options.explanation: Incorrect. While initially there are more products, the reaction will not yield more products. Instead, it will shift to form more reactants until equilibrium is reached.

options.text: The ratio increases to the value of Kc.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The equilibrium constant (Kc) is fixed at a given temperature and is not affected by the initial reaction quotient (Q). The ratio will adjust by shifting the reaction to reach equilibrium.
Incorrect. Equilibrium is reached when Q equals Kc. Here, Q is greater than Kc, indicating the system is not yet at equilibrium.

options.text: There is no shifting of reaction.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Since Q is greater than Kc, the reaction will shift in the reverse direction to decrease the concentration of products and increase the concentration of reactants until equilibrium is reached.

questions.explanation: If the reaction quotient Q > Kc , there are too many products compared to equilibrium. To reduce Q back to Kc , the system shifts toward reactants (reverse direction). Thus, the reaction moves backward until equilibrium is restored.

questions.text: For a reaction 2A + B ⇌ C + D the active mass of B is kept constant and that of A is tripled. It is observed that the rate of reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction rate is proportional to the active masses of the reactants. In this case, tripling the active mass of A leads to a ninefold increase in the reaction rate, indicating a second-order dependence on the concentration of A. Therefore this option is not correct.

The reaction rate is proportional to the active masses of the reactants. In this case, tripling the active mass of A leads to a ninefold increase in the reaction rate, indicating a second-order dependence on the concentration of A. Therefore this option is not correct.

The reaction rate is proportional to the active masses of the reactants. In this case, tripling the active mass of A leads to a ninefold increase in the reaction rate, indicating a second-order dependence on the concentration of A. Therefore this option is not correct.

According to the law of mass action, the rate equation for the above reaction is given as;Rate of reaction = k[A]² [B]¹ .It can be seen that the coefficients of each reactant become their powers. There are 2 moles of the reactant A in the reaction equation. Thus, the concentration of A would be squared in the rate equation ([A]²). Therefore, when the concentration of A is tripled, the rate would increase by 9 times (before increasing: [A]², after increasing: [3A]² = 9[A]²)Therefore only option D is the correct option.

questions.explanation: By the law of mass action, the rate ∝ (active mass of A)² × (active mass of B). Since B is constant and A is tripled, the rate becomes 3² = 9 times larger. Therefore, the reaction rate increases nine times.
hints: active mass of B is kept constant

questions.text: The change in concentration of reactant or product per unit time is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The rate constant is a proportionality factor in the rate equation that relates the reaction rate to the concentrations of reactants. It is not the same as the change in concentration per unit time.
The rate of reaction is defined as the change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time.
The rate equation is a mathematical expression that shows the dependence of reaction rate on the concentration of reactants, not the change in concentration per unit time.
The rate law is a mathematical relationship between the rate of a chemical reaction and the concentration of reactants. It does not define the rate of reaction itself.
Both the rate constant and rate law describe aspects of reaction kinetics but do not define the change in concentration per unit time.

questions.explanation: The rate of reaction measures how fast reactants are consumed or products are formed. It is defined as the change in concentration per unit time.Thus, option B is the correct definition.

questions.text: The equation N (g) + 3H (g) ⇋ 2NH (g) represents:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This process is primarily used to produce sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) from sulfur dioxide (SO₂). It does not involve nitrogen or hydrogen, making it unrelated to the reaction presented.

The correct name for the process represented by the equation is the Haber process, which synthesizes ammonia (NH₃) from nitrogen and hydrogen gases. However, this option is incorrectly labeled as a different process.
The Solvay process is used for producing sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) and does not involve the reaction of nitrogen and hydrogen to form ammonia. Therefore, it is not relevant to the equation given.
This option correctly identifies the reaction as the Haber process, which combines nitrogen (N₂) and hydrogen (H₂) gases to produce ammonia (NH₃) under high pressure and temperature.

questions.explanation: The reaction combines nitrogen and hydrogen to form ammonia (NH₃). This industrial method is specifically known as the Haber’s process. It is different from the Contact process (for H₂SO₄) and Solvay process (for Na₂CO₃).

questions.text: For a gaseous phase reaction, when number of moles of reactants and products are equal:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The values of K, and Kc are different
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. When the number of moles of reactants and products are equal, K and Kc are equivalent.

options.text: The value of K, is greater than K.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. K and Kc are related based on the conditions of the reaction, and in this scenario, they are equal when moles are balanced.

options.text: The value of K, is greater than Kp
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. K and Kp can differ depending on the change in moles of gas, but when moles are equal, they are the same.

options.text: The values of K, and Kc are the same
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. When the number of moles of reactants equals the number of moles of products, the equilibrium constants K and Kc are equal because Δn is zero.

questions.explanation: For gaseous reactions, K and K are related by𝐾𝑝 = 𝐾𝑐 (𝑅𝑇) Δ𝑛 , whereΔn is the change in moles of gas. If the number of moles of reactants equals products, then Δn=0. Thus, K = K in this case.

questions.text: The unit of Kc for the system N,O, = 2NO, is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because equilibrium constants do have units based on the concentrations of reactants and products.
This option contains an incorrect format for the units and does not accurately represent the correct units for Kc.
This is the correct option. The units for Kc are derived from the equilibrium expression and reflect the concentration of reactants and products.
This option is incorrect because it suggests a volume unit rather than the correct unit for Kc, which is related to concentration per molarity.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium expression is K = [NO₂]² / [N₂O₄]. The units are (mol·dm⁻³)² / (mol·dm⁻³) = mol·dm⁻³

questions.text: The addition of NaCl to AgCI decreases the solubility of AgCI:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect because the solubility product (Ksp) remains constant at a given temperature; rather, the solubility of AgCl is affected by the presence of common ions.
This option is misleading because the addition of NaCl does not lead to unsaturation; instead, it contributes to a saturated solution by reducing the solubility of AgCl.

options.text: Due to the common ion effect of Cl
options.explanation: This is the correct answer because the presence of Cl- from NaCl shifts the equilibrium of AgCl dissolution, decreasing its solubility through the common ion effect.

This option is incorrect because adding NaCl does not create a supersaturated solution; it instead lowers the solubility of AgCl, keeping the system at equilibrium without surpassing solubility limits.

questions.explanation: In the case of the common ion effect, the presence of a common ion (Cl ) in the solution decreases the solubility or ionization of the compound (AgCl) by shifting the equilibrium toward the formation of the undissociated or unionized form.

questions.text: Purification of table salt (NaCl) by passing HCl gas through its saturated aqueous solution is an example of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This law describes how the rate of a reaction is proportional to the concentration of reactants. While it is relevant to equilibrium, it does not directly explain the common ion effect observed in this scenario.

This law states that the total enthalpy change for a reaction is the sum of the enthalpy changes for individual steps. However, it does not pertain to the solubility changes when common ions are introduced.

The common ion effect describes how the solubility of a salt decreases when a common ion is added to the solution. In this case, adding Cl⁻ ions from HCl reduces the solubility of NaCl, exemplifying this principle.
Henry's law relates to the solubility of gases in liquids and does not apply to the ion interactions involved in the solubility of solid salts like NaCl in the presence of HCl.

questions.explanation: Adding HCl introduces extra Cl⁻ ions into the saturated NaCl solution. This suppresses the dissociation of NaCl, reducing its solubility. This phenomenon is called the common ion effect.

questions.text: All of the following are applications of K, EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It helps to predict the direction of the reaction.
options.explanation: The equilibrium constant indicates whether a reaction favors products or reactants at equilibrium, thus helping to predict its direction.

options.text: It tells about the extent of the reaction.
options.explanation: The equilibrium constant provides a quantitative measure of the concentrations of products and reactants at equilibrium, indicating how far a reaction proceeds.

options.text: It tells about the rate of reaction.
options.explanation: The equilibrium constant does not provide any information about the speed at which equilibrium is reached; this is the focus of chemical kinetics.
This option suggests that all statements are true; however, only options A and B accurately describe applications of K.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant (Kc ) provides information about the position of equilibrium (direction and extent) but gives no information about how fast the reaction reaches that equilibrium. The rate of reaction is the domain of chemical kinetics.

questions.text: For the reaction CO(g) + H₂O(g) ⇌ CO₂(g) + H₂(g), given ΔH° and ΔS° values at 300K and 1200K, the incorrect statement is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The reaction proceeds in the forward direction at 300 K
options.explanation: Option A assumes forward reaction at 300 K without checking ΔG°; it ignores thermodynamic calculations, making the statement unjustified generally.

options.text: At 1200 K, the reaction proceeds in the reverse direction
options.explanation: Option B claims reverse reaction at 1200 K, ignoring that Kp may exceed 1, making products still favored thermodynamically often.

By calculating the Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG° = ΔH° - TΔS°), the reaction is found to be non-spontaneous in the forward direction at 1200 K (ΔG° > 0). A non-spontaneous forward reaction means the equilibrium constant (Kp) must be less than 1, making statement C incorrect.

options.text: At 300 K, the products will be favored more than reactants at equilibrium
options.explanation: Option D mis-states equilibrium at 300 K, saying reactants are favored, whereas negative ΔG° favors products typically in reactions.

questions.explanation: By calculating the Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG° = ΔH° - TΔS°), the reaction is found to be non-spontaneous in the forward direction at 1200 K (ΔG° > 0). A non-spontaneous forward reaction means the equilibrium constant (Kp) must be less than 1, making statement C incorrect.

questions.text: At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: At equilibrium, the concentrations of both reactants and products remain unchanged over time, which means they are constant.
While the concentrations of reactants and products may initially be different, at equilibrium, their concentrations do not change, so this statement is misleading.
This option suggests that concentrations reach a maximum value, which is not accurate. At equilibrium, concentrations are stable, not necessarily at their maximum values.
This option implies that the concentrations of reactants and products are the same, which is not necessarily true. They can be constant but not equal.

questions.explanation: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction. This results in no net change in the concentrations of reactants and products, so their concentrations remain constant over time.

questions.text: Which of the following statements describes a reversible reaction at equilibrium?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Both forward and reverse reactions do not occur simultaneously
Both forward and reverse reactions occur simultaneously but under different conditions
Both forward and reverse reactions occur simultaneously under the same conditions
options.explanation: A key feature of dynamic equilibrium is that the forward and reverse reactions are occurring continuously and simultaneously at the same rate. This happens under a specific, constant set of conditions (temperature, pressure, etc.).

options.text: Forward and reverse reactions take place at different times and different conditions

questions.explanation: A key feature of dynamic equilibrium is that the forward and reverse reactions are occurring continuously and simultaneously at the same rate. This happens under a specific, constant set of conditions (temperature, pressure, etc.).

questions.text: For the homogeneous reaction, 4NH₃ + 5O₂ ⇌ 4NO + 6H₂O, the units of the equilibrium constant (K꜀) are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The units for K are determined by (concentration) , where Δn is the change in moles of gas (products - reactants). Here, Δn = (4+6) - (4+5) = 1. Therefore, the unit is (concentration)¹, or mol·dm⁻³.

questions.explanation: The units for K are determined by (concentration) , where Δn is the change in moles of gas (products - reactants). Here, Δn = (4+6) - (4+5) = 1. Therefore, the unit is (concentration)¹, or mol·dm⁻³.

questions.text: One mole of HI was sealed in a tube and heated to 440°C. At equilibrium, HI was found to be 50% dissociated. The Kc for the reaction 2HI ⇌ H₂ + I₂ is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: If 1 mole of HI is 50% dissociated, at equilibrium we have 0.5 mol HI, 0.25 mol H₂, and 0.25 mol I₂. K = [H₂][I₂]/[HI]² = (0.25) (0.25)/(0.5)² = 0.0625 / 0.25 = 0.25

questions.explanation: If 1 mole of HI is 50% dissociated, at equilibrium we have 0.5 mol HI, 0.25 mol H₂, and 0.25 mol I₂. K = [H₂][I₂]/[HI]² = (0.25) (0.25)/(0.5)² = 0.0625 / 0.25 = 0.25

questions.text: For what value of Ke the forward reaction is almost complete?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: If the Ke value is very low, the reaction is more favourable in the reverse direction. Hence, this option is not correct.
If the Ke value is very high, the reaction is more favourable in the forward direction. Hence, this option is correct.
If the Ke value is very high, the reaction is more favourable in the forward direction. This option does not show very high value, so this is not correct.
If the Ke value is low, the reaction is more favourable in the reverse direction. Hence, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: If the Ke value is very high, the reaction is more favourable in the forward direction. Hence, option b is correct.

questions.text: 1 mole of ethyl alcohol was treated with one mole of acetic acid at 25°C. 2/3 of the acid changes into ester at equilibrium. The equilibrium constant of the reaction will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This means products and reactants are equally favored. Only about half of the reactants would have turned into products. That does not match the given data because in our case, more than half (two-thirds) was converted.
This means products are slightly more favored than reactants, giving about 59% conversion to products. That is still less than the two-thirds conversion stated, so it is incorrect.
This means products are more favored than in option 2, giving about 63% conversion. This is close to two-thirds but still less, so it does not exactly match the given equilibrium condition.

This means products are clearly favored. It predicts exactly two-thirds of the reactants converting into products, which matches the problem statement perfectly. Therefore, this is the correct answer.

questions.text: At equilibrium, concentration of SO2 is 2M, O2 is 2M and SO3 is 4M.2SO2 +O2 ⇌ 2SO3 . What will be the Kc value of given reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is much smaller than the calculated Kc. It would mean the products are less favored compared to reactants, which is not the case here.
This is double the actual value. This would imply more products relative to reactants at equilibrium than actually given
This is four times the actual Kc Indicates an even greater product concentration compared to reactants, which contradicts the data.

questions.text: In a reaction: CO(g) + 2H₂(g) ⇌ CH₃OH(g) ∆H°= -92kJ/mol, if the concentrations of all species become constant, what will happen?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The equilibrium state will be disturbed
The equilibrium state will remain undisturbed
options.explanation: When the concentrations of all reactants and products become constant, it signifies that the system has reached a state of dynamic equilibrium. The forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, and unless conditions change, the state will remain undisturbed.

questions.explanation: When the concentrations of all reactants and products become constant, it signifies that the system has reached a state of dynamic equilibrium. The forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, and unless conditions change, the state will remain undisturbed.

questions.text: If Ke value is very small then equilibrium position will shift
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A very small Ke indicates that at equilibrium, the concentration of reactants is much higher than that of products, meaning the equilibrium position is towards the left.
If Ke were large, it would indicate a shift towards the right, favoring products. However, a small Ke does not support this.
The position of equilibrium always shifts depending on the conditions and the value of Ke. It does not remain unchanged when Ke is very small.
While Ke itself is constant at a given temperature, the position of equilibrium can vary. A small Ke indicates the equilibrium is towards the reactants, not a constant position.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant (Ke) provides insight into the position of equilibrium in a chemical reaction. A very small Ke value suggests that, at equilibrium, the concentration of reactants is much higher than that of the products, indicating that the equilibrium position is towards the left. This means the reaction favors the formation of reactants. Option A is correct as it reflects this understanding. Option B is incorrect because it describes a scenario where Ke is large, not small. Option C is incorrect because equilibrium does indeed shift based on Ke values. Option D confuses the constancy of Ke with equilibrium position, which is variable.

questions.text: For a reaction with the equilibrium expression K꜀ = [X]² / (V(a-[X])), what is true for this reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Increase in pressure will favor the forward reaction
Decrease in pressure will favor the forward reaction
options.explanation: The volume (V) in the denominator of the K expression indicates that the number of moles of products is greater than the number of moles of reactants. According to Le Chatelier's principle, a decrease in pressure will favor the direction that produces more moles of gas, which is the forward reaction.

options.text: Decrease in pressure will favor the backward reaction
Increase in volume will favor the backward reaction

questions.explanation: The volume (V) in the denominator of the K expression indicates that the number of moles of products is greater than the number of moles of reactants. According to Le Chatelier's principle, a decrease in pressure will favor the direction that produces more moles of gas, which is the forward reaction.

questions.text: For the reaction H2(g)+ I2(g) ⇌ 2HI(g),the equilibrium constant changes with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Total pressure does not directly affect the equilibrium constant for this reaction. The equilibrium constant is related to concentrations, not total pressure.
A catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant. It only speeds up the attainment of equilibrium but does not affect the position of the equilibrium.
While changes in the concentrations of H2 and I2 can affect the position of the equilibrium, it doesn't directly change the equilibrium constant itself.

Equilibrium constant (Kc) is ratio of Kf and Kr. its value is only changed by change in temperature. Change in temperature (∆T) changes both equilibrium constant (Kc) as well as equilibrium state/position (Qe).

questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant K changes with temperature.According to the Van’t Hoff equation, if the reaction is exothermic or endothermic, increasing or decreasing temperature shifts the equilibrium and changes K

questions.text: Correct relationship b/w Ke and Kp can be written as:

options.explanation: Missing T, so incomplete.Since Kp depends on RT, this is incorrect.
This is the correct relationship between ke and kp.
Incorrect order and exponent.
N is not standard here possibly Avogadro's number

questions.explanation: This equation simply converts between the concentration form of the equilibrium constant and the pressure form, by using the ideal gas law and accounting for the difference in the number of moles of gas between products and reactants. The (RT)Δn factor adjusts for how much “space” the gas molecules take up in pressure terms versus concentration terms.

questions.text: Consider the reaction given below: Which statement is true?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Reagent for I is KOH in alcohol
options.explanation: Reaction II is KOH in alcohol and reaction I is KOH in aqueous medium.

options.text: Reagent for II is KOH in aqueous medium

As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.
Elimination of primary alkyl halides gives alkenes, reaction II is KOH in alcohol and reaction I is KOH in aqueous medium.

questions.text: pH of a mixture which is 0.1 M CH3COOH and 0.05 M (CH3COO)2 Ba is [pKa of CH3COOH = 4.74]
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The pH of a mixture that is 0.1 M CH3COOH and 0.05 M (CH3COO)2 Ba can be calculated using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation:pH = pKa + log([A-] / [HA])where pKa is the dissociation constant of the acid, [A-] is the concentration of the conjugate base, and [HA] is the concentration of the acid.In this case, CH3COOH is the acid, and CH3COO- is the conjugate base. The dissociation constant (pKa) of CH3COOH is given as 4.74.The initial concentration of CH3COOH is 0.1 M, and the initial concentration of CH3COO- can be calculated as follows:[CH3COO-] = 2 * [CH3COO2Ba] = 2 * 0.05 M = 0.1 MSubstituting the values into the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, we get:pH = 4.74 + log(0.1 / 0.1)pH = 4.74 + log(1)pH = 4.74Therefore, the pH of the mixture is 4.74.
Would happen if salt concentration < acid concentration significantly
Would happen if salt concentration > acid concentration significantly.
Is just a round number, but doesn’t match the ratio here.

questions.explanation: Substituting the values into the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, we get:pH = 4.74 + log(0.1 / 0.1)pH = 4.74 + log(1)pH = 4.74Therefore, the pH of the mixture is 4.74.

This means Kp=Kc×(RT)−1=Kc/RTImplies Δn=−1, which doesn't fit this reaction.Incorrect.

questions.text: In the reaction A₂(g) + 4B₂(g) ⇌ 2AB₄(g) such that ∆H is negative, the formation of AB₄(g) will be favored at:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction is exothermic (ΔH is negative), so low temperature favors the forward reaction. The reaction goes from 5 moles of gas to 2 moles of gas, so high pressure favors the side with fewer moles, which is also the forward reaction.

questions.explanation: The reaction is exothermic (ΔH is negative), so low temperature favors the forward reaction. The reaction goes from 5 moles of gas to 2 moles of gas, so high pressure favors the side with fewer moles, which is also the forward reaction.

questions.text: For the reaction N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, where ∆H is negative, the forward reaction is favored by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's principle, for an exothermic reaction (ΔH is negative), decreasing the temperature will shift the equilibrium to the right to produce more heat, thus favoring the forward reaction.

questions.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's principle, for an exothermic reaction (ΔH is negative), decreasing the temperature will shift the equilibrium to the right to produce more heat, thus favoring the forward reaction.

Too high, unrealistic at equilibrium
The equilibrium mixture does not contain 55% ammonia.
Higher than typical equilibrium value

questions.explanation: Even at optimal temperature and pressure, the equilibrium favors only partial conversion.Industrially, around 15-20% NH₃ by volume is common in the equilibrium mixture before separation.

questions.text: The catalyst used in the Haber’s process is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is not correct because magnesium oxide is used in the dehydrohalogenation of halogenated hydrocarbons and alcohols.
This option is also incorrect because aluminum oxide is used in the dehydration of alcohols to an alkene.
This option is also incorrect because silicon oxide is used in the oxidation of benzene.

options.text: Iron crystals with metal oxide promoters
options.explanation: The catalyst used in the Haber's process is iron crystals with metal oxide promoters.

questions.explanation: Iron crystals with metal oxide promoters are used in the Haber process.Magnesium oxide is used in the dehydrohalogenation of halogenated hydrocarbons and alcohols.Aluminum oxide is used in the dehydration of alcohols to an alkene.Silicon oxide is used in the oxidation of benzene.

questions.text: What will happen to the KClO3 when KCl is added to its solution in water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because adding KCl introduces a common ion (Cl⁻), which suppresses KClO₃ solubility via the common‑ion effect.

When KCl is added to a solution of KClO₃, it introduces a common ion (K⁺) into the solution. Due to the common ion effect, the dissociation of KClO₃ is suppressed. As a result, the solubility of KClO₃ decreases, so the correct answer is B.
Option C is incorrect because adding KCl increases the total Cl⁻ concentration, not decreases it in the solution overall.
Option D is incorrect because KCl addition does not affect KClO₃ dissociation, so ClO₃⁻ concentration remains unchanged essentially.

questions.explanation: When KCl is added to a solution of KClO₃, it introduces a common ion (K⁺) into the solution. Due to the common ion effect, the dissociation of KClO₃ is suppressed. As a result, the solubility of KClO₃ decreases, so the correct answer is B.

questions.text: A chemical reaction is catalyzed by a catalyst X. Hence, X:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Increases activation energy of the reaction
options.explanation: A catalyst lowers activation energy, not increases it, enabling faster reactions by providing alternative pathways energetically.

options.text: Does not affect equilibrium constant of the reaction
options.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy, increasing the reaction rate. It does not change the energies of reactants or products, so the equilibrium position remains unchanged. Therefore, the correct answer is B .

options.text: Decreases rate constant of the reaction
options.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate constant by lowering activation energy, speeding up both forward and reverse reactions equally.
A catalyst does not affect reaction enthalpy (ΔH), which is a state function independent of reaction pathway or catalyst.

questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy, increasing the reaction rate. It does not change the energies of reactants or products, so the equilibrium position remains unchanged. Therefore, the correct answer is B .

questions.text: For the equilibrium reaction 2NO2 ⇌ N2O4 (g) (-61 kJ), increase of temperature would:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is wrong according to given reaction conditions.

If an exothermic reaction in equilibrium experiences a rise in temperature, the reaction shifts to the left toward reactants. An increase in temperature will favour the reverse reaction. So, N2O4 will undergo decomposition.
Option C is wrong according to given reaction conditions.
Option D is wrong according to given reaction conditions.

questions.explanation: The reaction is exothermic (ΔH = -61 kJ) in the forward direction (2NO₂ → N₂O₄). Increasing temperature adds heat, which the system counteracts by shifting toward the endothermic reverse reaction. Thus, the equilibrium shifts to decompose N₂O₄ into NO₂.

questions.text: Law of mass action was derived by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Law of mass action was not derived by Newton, so they are incorrect.
Law of mass action was not derived by this scientist, so they are incorrect.
Law of mass action was not derived by this scientists, so they are incorrect.
Peter Waage and Cato Guldberg both derived the law of mass action.

questions.explanation: The law of mass action states that the rate of a chemical reaction is proportional to the product of the concentrations of the reactants. It was formulated by the Norwegian chemists C.M. Guldberg and P. Waage in the 19th century.

questions.text: An excess of silver nitrate is added to the aqueous barium chloride and the precipitate is removed by filtration. What are the main ions in the filtrate?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: NO₃⁻ is a spectator ion and remains in solution.But what about Ba²⁺?Ba²⁺ ions were in the original BaCl₂ solution, but they do not react with AgNO₃ and do not form precipitates here.So Ba²⁺ ions remain dissolved.Therefore, Option A is incorrect because it excludes Ba²⁺.

options.text: Ag+ and NO3- and Ba2+ only.
options.explanation: Ag⁺: Excess silver ions left after all chloride precipitated.NO₃⁻: From silver nitrate, spectator ion.Ba²⁺: Present in the solution, unaffected.
It cannot be used as a main ions. so it will be incorrect.

options.text: CI- and NO3- and Ba2+ only.
options.explanation: Cl⁻ is removed as AgCl precipitate.So Cl⁻ concentration in filtrate should be nearly zero.This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The reaction is 2AgNO₃ + BaCl₂ → 2AgCl(s) + Ba(NO₃)₂. Since AgNO₃ is in excess, the unreacted Ag⁺ ions will be in the filtrate along with the spectator Ba²⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions. The Cl⁻ ions are completely precipitated as AgCl.

questions.text: The solubility product is only applicable for those substance which are :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: At a concentration of 0.1 M, the dissolved ions are relatively concentrated.When ionic strength is high (like 0.1 M), ionic interactions become significant, and the solution deviates from ideal behavior.
The solubility product is applicable for sparingly soluble salt whose concentration is equal to or less than 0.01 mol dm-3.
At 1 M concentration, the solution is very concentrated. Strong ionic interactions and high ionic strength cause large deviations from ideality.
Concentrations greater than 0.01 M start to move beyond the dilute solution range. Non-ideal behavior increases, and the accuracy of Ksp without considering activity coefficients decrease.

questions.explanation: The concept of the solubility product constant (Ksp ) is specifically used to describe the equilibrium of sparingly soluble or nearly insoluble ionic compounds. For highly soluble salts, the ion concentrations are too high for the simple Ksp equilibrium to hold true.

questions.text: The molar solubility of sparingly soluble salt AB3 is "s" mol/dm3, the corresponding solubility product Ksp is given in term of Ksp by the reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A: ignores stoichiometry of AB₃ dissociation.
B: uses wrong coefficient for B⁻³ ions.
The dissociation is AB₃ ⇌ A³⁺ + 3B⁻. At equilibrium, [A³⁺] = S and [B⁻] = 3S. The solubility product is Ksp = [A³⁺][B⁻]³ = (S) (3S)³ = S(27S³) = 27S⁴.
D: uses incorrect power for solubility expression.

questions.explanation: The dissociation is AB₃ ⇌ A³⁺ + 3B⁻. At equilibrium, [A³⁺] = S and [B⁻] = 3S. The solubility product is Ksp = [A³⁺][B⁻]³ = (S) (3S)³ = S(27S³) = 27S⁴.

questions.text: For a reversible reaction the value of Kc is 4, if the concentration of products is reduced to half, then the value of Kc for that reaction at constant temperature will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The value of Kc is not affected by changes in concentration of reactants or products as long as the temperature remains constant.
This would imply that Kc decreases in a non-proportional way with concentration changes, which is not true.Changing product concentration affects the reaction quotient Q, not Kc.
This suggests a partial change in Kc, which again is wrong — Kc is independent of how much product or reactant you currently have at the moment.
Kc stays at 4 because it depends only on temperature for a given reaction.If you reduce products to half, the reaction will shift forward to make more products, but the Kc value itself won’t change.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant (Kc ) for a given reaction depends only on temperature. Changing the concentrations of reactants or products will shift the equilibrium position to re establish the same Kc value, but Kc itself does not change

questions.text: Which reaction is favored at low pressure?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Low pressure favors more moles; ammonia synthesis decreases moles, so reaction shifts backward, not favored.
Equal moles on both sides; pressure change has no effect, so it isn't favored specifically at low pressure.

According to Le Chatelier's principle, decreasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium to the side with a greater number of moles of gas. In this reaction, 1 mole of gas decomposes into 2 moles of gas, so it is favored by low pressure.
Equal moles on both sides; pressure change has no effect, so it isn't favored specifically at low pressure.

questions.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's principle, decreasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium to the side with a greater number of moles of gas. In this reaction, 1 mole of gas decomposes into 2 moles of gas, so it is favored by low pressure.

questions.text: The solubility of A2X3 is ''s'' mol.dm-3, its solubility product is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This ignores the stoichiometric coefficients and powers from dissociation.
This comes from an incorrect assumption about ion ratios (probably for a 1:2 salt like AB₂).
The X⁵ power is correct, but the coefficient is wrong because it doesn’t multiply 4 and 27 correctly.
The dissociation is A₂X₃ ⇌ 2A³⁺ + 3X²⁻. At equilibrium, [A³⁺] = 2s and [X²⁻] = 3s. The solubility product is Ksp = [A³⁺]²[X²⁻]³ = (2s)²(3s)³ = (4s²)(27s³) = 108s⁵.

questions.explanation: The dissociation is A₂X₃ ⇌ 2A³⁺ + 3X²⁻. At equilibrium, [A³⁺] = 2s and [X²⁻] = 3s. The solubility product is Ksp = [A³⁺]²[X²⁻]³ = (2s)²(3s)³ = (4s²)(27s³) = 108s⁵.

questions.text: In the reaction; H2 + CO2 ⇌ H2O + CO. The decrease in the concentration of reactants and products cause the equilibrium to shift:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction will move towards left if the concentration of reactants is decreased.
It will move towards right if concentration of product will decrease.
Since both sides concentration is decreasing, hence there will be no effect on concentration.

options.text: Equilibrium will shift towards both the directions
options.explanation: This is not possible that equilibrium shifts in both directions.

questions.explanation: At equilibrium, the system depends on the ratio of concentrations (the reaction quotient), not the absolute amounts. A proportional decrease in both reactants and products does not change the ratio, so the system remains at equilibrium. Thus, the equilibrium does not shift.

questions.text: The equilibrium expression for a reaction is Kc = x2/V(a - x). What is true for the reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Increase of pressure favors the forward reaction.
options.explanation: Pressure increase favors fewer moles; here forward makes more moles, so A is wrong explanation simply.

options.text: Decrease of pressure favors the backward reaction.
options.explanation: Pressure decrease favors more moles; it should favor forward, not backward, simple mistake.

options.text: Decrease of pressure favors the forward reaction.
options.explanation: The volume (V) in the denominator indicates that the number of moles of products is greater than the number of moles of reactants. According to Le Chatelier's principle, decreasing the pressure will favor the side with more moles of gas, which is the forward reaction.

options.text: Increase of volume favors the backward reaction.
options.explanation: Volume increase equals pressure decrease; it favors forward reaction, not backward, basic error.

questions.explanation: The volume (V) in the denominator indicates that the number of moles of products is greater than the number of moles of reactants. According to Le Chatelier's principle, decreasing the pressure will favor the side with more moles of gas, which is the forward reaction.

questions.text: A chemical reaction in which product formed are stable at the temperature at which reaction is taking place is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: .Products form and remain stable; backward reaction is insignificant.
Products are stable and don’t revert back.
Would mean products are unstable and revert to reactants.
In a reversible reaction, products and reactants interconvert; stability of products alone doesn’t define this.

questions.explanation: An irreversible reaction is one that proceeds in a single direction until the reactants are essentially consumed. The products are stable under the reaction conditions and do not readily convert back to reactants, effectively making the process one-way.

questions.text: When the number of moles of reactants and products are equal in reversible reaction which parameter would not affect at fusion equilibrium?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Temperature changes can shift the equilibrium position by altering the rates of endothermic and exothermic reactions.
In reactions where the number of moles of reactants and products are equal, pressure changes do not affect the equilibrium position.
This option is incorrect as pressure does not affect equilibrium under the given condition.
A catalyst speeds up the reaction rate but does not change the equilibrium position itself.

questions.explanation: When the number of moles of reactants and products is equal, changes in pressure do not affect the equilibrium position because there is no net change in volume. Temperature can affect equilibrium because it can alter the balance of endothermic and exothermic reactions. A Catalyst speeds up the rate of a reaction but does not change the equilibrium position.

questions.text: Exothermic reactions are favoured in the forward direction by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Exothermic reactions are favoured in the forward direction by decreasing the temperature. This is because exothermic reactions release heat, so decreasing the temperature will help to drive the reaction forward by removing heat from the system.

Endothermic reactions are favoured in the forward direction by increasing the temperature. This is because endothermic reactions absorb heat, so increasing the temperature will help to drive the reaction forward by providing heat to the system.

Exothermic reactions are not favoured in the forward direction by adding a catalyst. Catalysts do not affect the position of equilibrium, so they do not affect whether an exothermic reaction proceeds in the forward or reverse direction.A catalyst is a substance that speeds up the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being consumed in the reaction. Catalysts work by providing a different pathway for the reaction to take place, a pathway that has a lower activation energy. This means that the reaction can proceed more quickly, but it does not change the equilibrium of the reaction.
Le Chatelier's Principle states that a system in equilibrium will shift in the direction that counteracts any change that is made to the system.

questions.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's principle, if a change is applied to a system at equilibrium, the system will shift to counteract the change. For an exothermic reaction (which releases heat), decreasing the temperature will shift the equilibrium to the right to produce more heat.

questions.text: Chemical equilibrium is established in:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Chemical equilibrium is established in a system where the forward and reverse reactions are occurring at the same rate. This means that there is no net change in the concentrations of the reactants and products over time.The equilibrium state is a dynamic state, meaning that the forward and reverse reactions are still occurring, but they are occurring at the same rate so there is no net change in the system.The equilibrium state can be affected by changes in temperature, pressure, or concentration. If any of these factors are changed, the equilibrium state will shift to a new position.

An irreversible reaction is a reaction that proceeds in one direction only and cannot be reversed. This means that the reactants are converted into products and the products cannot be converted back into the reactants.
Only A is the correct answer as explained above.

A forward reaction is the reaction that proceeds from the reactants to the products. In an irreversible reaction, the forward reaction proceeds to completion, meaning that all of the reactants are converted into products.

questions.explanation: Chemical equilibrium is a state that is reached only in reversible reactions. It is the point at which the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction, resulting in no net change in concentrations.

questions.text: In which of the following reaction Kc has moles-2 dm+6 unit?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Unit for this reaction is: [NH3]2 / [N2][H2]3 (mol dm-3)2 / (mol dm-3) (mol dm-3)3 = (mol2dm-6) / (mol4dm-12) = (mol-2dm6)Hence, this option is correct.
The unit for this reaction is [PCl3] [Cl2] / [PCl5] (mol dm-3)(mol dm-3) / (mol dm-3) = (mol2dm-6) / (mol dm-3) = (mol dm-3)Hence, this option is incorrect.
The unit for this reaction is [CH3COOC2H5] / [CH3COOH] [C2H5OH] (mol dm-3) / (mol dm-3) (mol dm-3) = (mol dm-3)/ (mol2dm-6) = (mol-1dm3)Hence, this option is incorrect.
The unit for this reaction is [SO3]2 / [SO2] [O2] (mol dm-3)2 / (mol dm-3) (mol dm-3) = (mol2dm-6) / (mol2dm-6)= no unit (both cancel each other)Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The units for K are (concentration) . For this reaction, Δn = (products) - (reactants) = 2 - (1+3) = -2. Therefore, the units are (mol·dm⁻³), which is mol⁻²·dm⁶.

questions.text: The product of the concentrations of each ion in a saturated solution of a sparingly soluble salt at 298 K, raised to the power of their relative concentrations, is the:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Solubility product, Ksp, is the mathematical product of the dissolved ions concentration raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients.
An acid dissociation constant (Ka ) is a quantitative measure of the strength of an acid in solution
The equilibrium constant, Kw, is called the dissociation constant or ionization constant of water.

Kb is the base dissociation constant. The base dissociation constant is a measure of how completely a base dissociates into its component ions in water. The equilibrium constant, Kw, is called the dissociation constant or ionization constant of water.

questions.explanation: This is the definition of the solubility product constant (Ksp ). It represents the equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound into its constituent ions in a saturated solution.
hints: product of the concentrations of each ion in a saturated solution

questions.text: A graph showing concentration decreasing over time and approaching a non-zero constant value could represent the concentration of a:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a reversible reaction the product’s concentration increases over time and reaches a non‑zero equilibrium value. The question describes a decreasing concentration, so this option does not fit.

The graph shows a decreasing concentration, characteristic of a reactant. Since the concentration levels off at a value greater than zero, it indicates that the reaction reaches equilibrium and does not go to completion. This is characteristic of a reactant in a reversible reaction.
In an irreversible reaction the product’s concentration increases until the reactant is fully consumed (or the reaction goes to completion). It does not decrease over time.
For an irreversible reaction the reactant’s concentration decreases to zero as the reaction goes to completion, not to a non‑zero constant.

questions.explanation: The graph shows a decreasing concentration, characteristic of a reactant. Since the concentration levels off at a value greater than zero, it indicates that the reaction reaches equilibrium and does not go to completion. This is characteristic of a reactant in a reversible reaction.

questions.text: For which reaction is volume an irrelevant factor in determining the equilibrium position?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Changes in pressure or volume only affect the equilibrium position of reactions involving gases where there is a change in the total number of moles of gas. The esterification reaction shown occurs in the liquid phase, so pressure and volume changes have a negligible effect.
B is wrong because it has different gas molecule counts, so volume affects balance shifting reactions.
C is wrong because it has different gas molecule counts, so volume changes move the reaction position.
D is wrong because it has different gas molecule counts, so volume influences the equilibrium shifting.

questions.explanation: Changes in pressure or volume only affect the equilibrium position of reactions involving gases where there is a change in the total number of moles of gas. The esterification reaction shown occurs in the liquid phase, so pressure and volume changes have a negligible effect.

questions.text: Precipitation occurs when:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ksp < ionic product: In a precipitation reaction, if the solubility product constant (Ksp) is less than the ionic product, then the reaction is not at equilibrium, and a precipitation will occur. This means that the concentration of the ions in the solution exceeds the maximum solubility, causing the excess to precipitate as a solid.

Ksp > ionic product: If the solubility product constant (Ksp) is greater than the ionic product, then the reaction is not at equilibrium, and no precipitation will occur. In this case, the concentration of the ions in the solution is below the maximum solubility, and the solution remains clear without any precipitation.

Ksp = ionic product: When the solubility product constant (Ksp) is equal to the ionic product, the reaction is at equilibrium. At this point, the concentration of the ions in the solution is exactly at the maximum solubility, and there is no net formation or dissolution of the solid. This condition indicates that the solution is saturated with the solute, and no precipitation will occur.

√Ksp < ionic product: This option suggests that the square root of the solubility product constant (√Ksp) is less than the ionic product. However, this representation is not commonly used in discussing precipitation reactions and is not directly related to the conditions for precipitation.

questions.explanation: The ionic product (Qsp ) is the product of ion concentrations at any given moment, while the solubility product (Ksp ) is the value at saturation. If the ionic product exceeds the Ksp , the solution is supersaturated, and the excess solute will precipitate out until Qsp = Ksp .

questions.text: For X+Y ⇌ Z Ke= 6x10-7 it means that at equilibrium:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. A very small Ke means the equilibrium lies far to the left, so products are not favored.
Partially correct but incomplete since Y is also a reactant and would be favored alongside X

options.text: X and Y would be favored
options.explanation: Correct. Small Ke​ means reactants (X and Y) predominate at equilibrium.

X, Y, Z would be favored: This option suggests that at equilibrium, the concentrations of X, Y, and Z would all be favored, which means they would all be present at higher concentrations. However, this is not correct based on the given equilibrium constant.

questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant (Kc ) is the ratio of products to reactants. A very small K value (much less than 1) indicates that at equilibrium, the concentration of reactants is significantly greater than the concentration of the product. Therefore, the reactants (X and Y) are favored.

questions.text: Which of the following can be regarded as a favorable condition for ammonia synthesis (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, ΔH < 0)?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Synthesis of ammonia is an exothermic process. At high temperature reverse reaction will be favoured and yield of ammonia will decrease. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Low pressure will shift the reaction in the direction having greater number of moles. In case of ammonia synthesis low pressure will favour reverse reaction due to which the yield will decrease. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Although low temperature will favour ammonia production but low pressure, as explained above, will favour reverse reaction and the yield will decrease.

This option is correct. As ammonia synthesis is an exothermic process, so low temperature will favour forward reaction to counteract the change and increase the temperature. Moreover, high pressure will shift reaction in the direction having fewer number of moles. In case of ammonia, the reaction will shift in the forward direction. As a result of these two changes, production of ammonia will be favoured.

questions.explanation: .The reaction is exothermic, so low temperature favors the product. The reaction proceeds from 4 moles of gas to 2 moles, so high pressure favors the side with fewer moles (the product). Therefore, low temperature and high pressure are the favorable conditions.

questions.text: For the reaction A + B ⇌ C, K = 4.0. If at some point the reaction quotient Q = 2, then:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A says products should react back, but Qc < Kc means the system makes more products.
B says reactants should form, implying Qc > Kc, but actually Qc < Kc pushes the reaction forward.

Since the reaction quotient (Qc = 2) is less than the equilibrium constant (Kc = 4), the ratio of products to reactants is too small. To reach equilibrium, the reaction must proceed in the forward direction, meaning more reactants must react to form more products.
D claims the reaction is at equilibrium, which requires Qc = Kc, but Qc ≠ Kc here.

questions.explanation: Since the reaction quotient (Qc = 2) is less than the equilibrium constant (Kc = 4), the ratio of products to reactants is too small. To reach equilibrium, the reaction must proceed in the forward direction, meaning more reactants must react to form more products.

questions.text: Considering the influence of a catalyst on a chemical equilibrium, which one of the following is wrong? The catalyst:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Increases the speed of both forward and reverse reactions to the same extent
options.explanation: Yes, a catalyst increases the rate of both the forward and reverse reactions to the same extent. A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without itself being consumed in the reaction. Catalysts work by providing an alternative pathway for the reaction to occur. This pathway has a lower activation energy than the uncatalyzed pathway, which means that the reaction can proceed more quickly. When a catalyst is added to a reversible reaction, it increases the rate of both the forward and reverse reactions. This is because the catalyst provides an alternative pathway for both reactions to occur.

options.text: Does not change the relative amount of substances present at equilibrium
options.explanation: A catalyst does not change the relative amount of substances present at equilibrium. A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction without being consumed by the reaction. Catalysts work by providing an alternative pathway for the reaction to take place. This pathway has a lower activation energy than the uncatalyzed pathway, which means that the reaction can proceed faster. However, the catalyst does not change the overall change in free energy of the reaction. This means that the catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant of the reaction. The equilibrium constant is a measure of the relative amounts of reactants and products at equilibrium.

options.text: Changes the time required for reaching the equilibrium
options.explanation: Yes, a catalyst changes the time required for reaching equilibrium. A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being consumed in the reaction. A catalyst does this by providing an alternative pathway for the reaction to occur, which has a lower activation energy. The activation energy is the minimum amount of energy that the reactants must have in order to react. A catalyst lowers the activation energy by providing a surface on which the reactants can interact. This makes it easier for the reactants to react and reach equilibrium more quickly.
The equilibrium constant K depends only on temperature. A catalyst does not affect K. It only affects the rate, not the equilibrium position.

questions.explanation: A catalyst does not change the equilibrium constant (K) or the position of equilibrium. It only increases the rate at which both the forward and reverse reactions occur, allowing the system to reach equilibrium faster.

questions.text: For the reaction N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), if K = 0.04 at 500 K and the concentrations are [N₂] = 0.2 M, [H₂] = 0.3 M, [NH₃] = 0.1 M, what is the reaction quotient Q?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A (0.02) is incorrect because it is much smaller than the actual Qc.
B (0.04) is incorrect because it is also much smaller than the actual Qc and does not match the calculated value.
D (0.16) is incorrect because it is significantly lower than the computed Qc.

questions.text: For the reaction: N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3 .The production of NH3 will be favored at:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: To determine the favored conditions, you can apply Le Chatelier's Principle. Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Since there are fewer moles on the right (2 moles of NH3) compared to the left (4 moles of N2 and 6 moles of H2), higher pressure would favor the production of NH3.

To determine the favored conditions, you can apply Le Chatelier's Principle. Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Since there are fewer moles on the right (2 moles of NH3) compared to the left (4 moles of N2 and 6 moles of H2), higher pressure would favor the production of NH3. There this option is not correct.

To determine the favored conditions, you can apply Le Chatelier's Principle. Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Since there are fewer moles on the right (2 moles of NH3) compared to the left (4 moles of N2 and 6 moles of H2), higher pressure would favor the production of NH3. Therefore this option is not correct.

To determine the favored conditions, you can apply Le Chatelier's Principle. Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Since there are fewer moles on the right (2 moles of NH3) compared to the left (4 moles of N2 and 6 moles of H2), higher pressure would favor the production of NH3.

To determine the favored conditions, you can apply Le Chatelier's Principle. Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Since there are fewer moles on the right (2 moles of NH3) compared to the left (4 moles of N2 and 6 moles of H2), higher pressure would favor the production of NH3. Therefore this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The forward reaction forms fewer moles of gas (4 → 2), so high pressure shifts equilibrium toward NH₃. A catalyst increases the reaction rate but does not change equilibrium position. Therefore, maximum ammonia production is achieved using high pressure along with a catalyst.

questions.text: For the reaction 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO(g), if Kp = 2.5 × 10³ at 298 K, what is Kc? (R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A is wrong because 6.1 × 10¹ is too low, ignoring proper Δn effect in Kp to Kc conversion formula totally.
B is wrong because 1.0 × 10² misses the exponential Kp‑Kc relation, misusing gas constant and temperature factors badly.
C is wrong because 2.5 × 10³ is the Kp value, not Kc, forgetting Δn adjustment in equilibrium conversion step.

questions.text: For the reaction PCl₅(g) ⇌ PCl₃(g) + Cl₂(g), an increase in pressure will:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A is incorrect because increasing pressure favors the side with fewer gas moles, shifting the equilibrium backward here.
Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Here, the reverse reaction (2 moles → 1 mole) is favored.
C is incorrect because pressure affects equilibria with gaseous species, so it does influence this reaction's position.
D is incorrect because pressure changes shift equilibrium position, but they do not alter the equilibrium constant Kc value.

questions.explanation: Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. Here, the reverse reaction (2 moles → 1 mole) is favored.

questions.text: The solubility product (Ksp) of AgCl is 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰. What is the molar solubility of AgCl?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A (1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ M): Incorrect because solubility equals square root of Ksp, not Ksp itself, yielding higher concentration than given Ksp.
C (3.6 × 10⁻⁵ M): Incorrect because squaring this solubility gives Ksp = 1.3 × 10⁻⁹, exceeding given Ksp value significantly.
D (9.0 × 10⁻¹¹ M): Incorrect because squaring this solubility yields Ksp = 8.1 × 10⁻²¹, far below the given Ksp value.

questions.text: For the reaction 2HI(g) ⇌ H₂(g) + I₂(g), if 1.0 mol of HI is 40% dissociated at equilibrium, what is Kc?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Initial [HI] = 1.0 mol. At 40% dissociation, [HI] = 0.6 mol, [H₂] = [I₂] = 0.2 mol. Kc = [H₂][I₂] / [HI]² = (0.2 × 0.2) / (0.6)² = 0.04 / 0.36 ≈ 0.11.
B (0.25): Value exceeds calculated Kc; implies higher dissociation than actual 40%, misrepresenting equilibrium concentrations given.
C (0.40): Overestimates Kc; suggests dissociation greater than observed, mismatching HI and product concentrations calculated.
D (0.67): Kc value too high; indicates excessive product formation, inconsistent with 40% HI dissociation data provided.

questions.explanation: Initial [HI] = 1.0 mol. At 40% dissociation, [HI] = 0.6 mol, [H₂] = [I₂] = 0.2 mol. Kc = [H₂][I₂] / [HI]² = (0.2 × 0.2) / (0.6)² = 0.04 / 0.36 ≈ 0.11.

questions.text: A chemical engineer wants to use the Haber process in order to produce ammonia as raw material for fertilizers. Which of the following parameters must the chemical engineer AVOID in order to obtain the maximum yield of ammonia?N2(g) + 3H2(g) -> 2NH3(g) ΔH0 = -92.6 kJ/mol
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's Principle, increasing the temperature will shift the equilibrium of the exothermic Haber process to the left, which will result in a lower yield of ammonia. Therefore, to obtain the maximum yield of ammonia, the chemical engineer should avoid increasing the temperature.

Using a catalyst speeds up the reaction without being consumed in the process. It does not affect the equilibrium position or the yield of ammonia. Therefore, using a catalyst is beneficial and can be employed to maximize the ammonia yield.

According to Le Chatelier's principle, adding more reactants (nitrogen gas) will shift the reaction towards the products (ammonia) to counteract the increase. This will result in a higher yield of ammonia. Therefore, adding more nitrogen gas can help maximize the ammonia yield.

Increasing the pressure favors the side of the reaction with fewer gas molecules. In this case, increasing the pressure will favor the formation of ammonia. Therefore, increasing the pressure can help maximize the ammonia yield.

questions.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's Principle, increasing the temperature will shift the equilibrium of the exothermic Haber process to the left, which will result in a lower yield of ammonia. Therefore, to obtain the maximum yield of ammonia, the chemical engineer should avoid increasing the temperature.

questions.text: For the reaction N₂O₄(g) ⇌ 2NO₂(g), ΔH > 0, which change will shift the equilibrium to the right?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A (Increasing pressure) is incorrect because the reaction increases moles of gas from 1 to 2, so higher pressure favors the reverse reaction, shifting left.
Option B (Decreasing temperature) is incorrect because the reaction is endothermic (ΔH > 0), so lowering temperature favors the exothermic reverse reaction, shifting left.
For an endothermic reaction (ΔH > 0), increasing temperature favors the forward reaction per Le Chatelier’s principle.
Option D (Adding a catalyst) is incorrect because a catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse rates equally, so it does not shift the equilibrium position at all.

questions.explanation: For an endothermic reaction (ΔH > 0), increasing temperature favors the forward reaction per Le Chatelier’s principle.

questions.text: What is the Ksp expression for Ca₃(PO₄)₂?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A: ignores stoichiometry, treats Ca₃(PO₄)₂ like a 1:1 salt, missing ion powers, wrong expression totally simple.
Option B: reverses the ion powers, uses 2 for calcium and 3 for phosphate, opposite of actual stoichiometry wrongly.
Ca₃(PO₄)₂ ⇌ 3Ca²⁺ + 2PO₄³⁻, Ksp = [Ca²⁺]³[PO₄³⁻]².
Option D: uses wrong calcium power of 1 instead of 3, phosphate power is correct but overall expression is incorrect totally.

questions.explanation: Ca₃(PO₄)₂ ⇌ 3Ca²⁺ + 2PO₄³⁻, Ksp = [Ca²⁺]³[PO₄³⁻]².

questions.text: Which of the following favors the formation of products in the reaction: CO(g) + H₂O(g) ⇌ CO₂(g) + H₂(g)?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: It is incorrect because adding CO increases reactant, shifting equilibrium left, reducing products formation.
Removing H₂ shifts the equilibrium to the right to produce more H₂, favoring products.
It is incorrect because increasing pressure has no effect on equal mole gaseous reaction, no shift occurs.
It is incorrect because adding catalyst speeds both directions equally, not favoring product formation specifically.

questions.explanation: Removing H₂ shifts the equilibrium to the right to produce more H₂, favoring products.

questions.text: N> 04 = 2NO3, Kc = 4. This reversible reaction is studied graphically as shown in the figure. Select the correct statements:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Reaction quotient has maximum value at point A
options.explanation: Option A is wrong because reaction quotient Q is not maximum at point A, it starts high initially.

options.text: Reaction proceeds right to left at a point when [N2O4] = [NO2] = 0.1M
options.explanation: Option B is wrong because when N2O4 equals NO2, Q is less than Kc, reaction goes forward not left.

options.text: Kc = Q when point A or C is reached
options.explanation: Option C is wrong because Kc equals Q only at equilibrium, not at points A or C during reaction.

options.text: Reaction quotient has minimum value at a point B
options.explanation: At the start of a reaction, products are zero or very low, so Q = [products]/[reactants] is small. As the reaction proceeds, products increase and reactants decrease, so Q increases. Q reaches its maximum when the reaction nears equilibrium, depending on product/reactant ratios.

questions.explanation: At the start of a reaction, products are zero or very low, so Q = [products]/[reactants] is small. As the reaction proceeds, products increase and reactants decrease, so Q increases. Q reaches its maximum when the reaction nears equilibrium, depending on product/reactant ratios.
